Data Science and Business Analytics
Ensemble Techniques and Model Tuning
Visa Approval Facilitation

Problem Statement¶

Context¶

Business communities in the United States are facing high demand for human resources, but one of the constant challenges is identifying and attracting the right talent, which is perhaps the most important element in remaining competitive. Companies in the United States look for hard-working, talented, and qualified individuals both locally as well as abroad.

The Immigration and Nationality Act (INA) of the US permits foreign workers to come to the United States to work on either a temporary or permanent basis. The act also protects US workers against adverse impacts on their wages or working conditions by ensuring US employers' compliance with statutory requirements when they hire foreign workers to fill workforce shortages. The immigration programs are administered by the Office of Foreign Labor Certification (OFLC).

OFLC processes job certification applications for employers seeking to bring foreign workers into the United States and grants certifications in those cases where employers can demonstrate that there are not sufficient US workers available to perform the work at wages that meet or exceed the wage paid for the occupation in the area of intended employment.

Objective¶

In FY 2016, the OFLC processed 775,979 employer applications for 1,699,957 positions for temporary and permanent labor certifications. This was a nine percent increase in the overall number of processed applications from the previous year. The process of reviewing every case is becoming a tedious task as the number of applicants is increasing every year.

The increasing number of applicants every year calls for a Machine Learning based solution that can help in shortlisting the candidates having higher chances of VISA approval. OFLC has hired the firm EasyVisa for data-driven solutions. You as a data scientist at EasyVisa have to analyze the data provided and, with the help of a classification model:

  • Facilitate the process of visa approvals.
  • Recommend a suitable profile for the applicants for whom the visa should be certified or denied based on the drivers that significantly influence the case status.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of employee and the employer. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • case_id: ID of each visa application
  • continent: Information of continent the employee
  • education_of_employee: Information of education of the employee
  • has_job_experience: Does the employee has any job experience? Y= Yes; N = No
  • requires_job_training: Does the employee require any job training? Y = Yes; N = No
  • no_of_employees: Number of employees in the employer's company
  • yr_of_estab: Year in which the employer's company was established
  • region_of_employment: Information of foreign worker's intended region of employment in the US.
  • prevailing_wage: Average wage paid to similarly employed workers in a specific occupation in the area of intended employment. The purpose of the prevailing wage is to ensure that the foreign worker is not underpaid compared to other workers offering the same or similar service in the same area of employment.
  • unit_of_wage: Unit of prevailing wage. Values include Hourly, Weekly, Monthly, and Yearly.
  • full_time_position: Is the position of work full-time? Y = Full Time Position; N = Part Time Position
  • case_status: Flag indicating if the Visa was certified or denied

Note: This is a sample solution for the project. Projects will NOT be graded on the basis of how well the submission matches this sample solution. Projects will be graded on the basis of the rubric only.¶

In [174]:
# Libraries to help with reading and manipulating data
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Libaries to help with data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# To tune model, get different metric scores, and split data
from sklearn.metrics import (
    f1_score,
    accuracy_score,
    recall_score,
    precision_score,
    confusion_matrix,
    roc_auc_score,
    ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
)
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score

# To be used for data scaling and one hot encoding
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, OneHotEncoder

# To impute missing values
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn import metrics

# To oversample and undersample data
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

# To do hyperparameter tuning
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV

# To define maximum number of columns to be displayed in a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)

# To supress scientific notations for a dataframe
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To help with model building
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import (
    AdaBoostClassifier,
    GradientBoostingClassifier,
    RandomForestClassifier,
    BaggingClassifier,
)
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# To suppress scientific notations
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: "%.3f" % x)

# To supress warnings
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
no_of_employees 25480.000 5667.043 22877.929 -26.000 1022.000 2109.000 3504.000 602069.000

  • no_of_employees: The average number of employees is about 5,667. At least 75% of observations have 3,504 employees. There is a huge difference between the 75th percentile and the max indicating outliers.
  • yr_of_estab:The average established year of the companies observed was 1979 and the most recently established company was in 2016.
  • prevailing_wage:The average prevailing wage is 74,455. The min is 2.13 and the max is about 319,210. This raw stat doesn't tell us much because the frequency of payment is unknown.

Converting prevailing_wage into annual amounts for comparability

In [184]:
# Creating a conversion dictionary
conversion = {
    'Hour': 40 * 52,   # 40 hours * 52 weeks
    'Week': 52,
    'Month': 12,
    'Year': 1
}
# Converting all wage types to annual
data['annual_wage'] = data['prevailing_wage'] * data['unit_of_wage'].map(conversion)

# Updating prevailing_wage column
data['prevailing_wage'] = data['annual_wage']
data = data.drop(columns=['annual_wage'])

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Leading Questions¶

1. What is the distribution of visa case statuses (certified vs. denied)?

In [189]:
# Count certified vs denied
case_counts = data['case_status'].value_counts()
labels = case_counts.index
values = case_counts.values
colors = ['#2ecc71', '#e74c3c']  # green / red

plt.figure(figsize=(6,5))
bars = plt.bar(labels, values, color=colors)

# Add percentage labels
total = values.sum()
for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()
    percentage = f"{100 * height / total:.1f}%"
    plt.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height,
        percentage,
        ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=11
    )

plt.xlabel("Case Status")
plt.ylabel("Number of Cases")
plt.title("Visa Case Outcomes: Certified vs Denied")
plt.show()
  • About 67% of the cases were certified and about 33% were denied.

2. How does the education level of employees impact visa approval rates?

In [190]:
edu_status_counts = data.groupby(['education_of_employee', 'case_status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
edu_status_pct = edu_status_counts.div(edu_status_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0) * 100

edu_status_pct.plot(kind='bar', stacked=True, figsize=(12, 6))

plt.title('Certified vs Denied Visas by Education Level (%) - Stacked')
plt.xlabel('Education Level')
plt.ylabel('Percentage (%)')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.legend(title='Case Status')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Employees with Doctorates had the greatest percentage of approved Visas at about 90%, followed by Master's at ~80%, Bachelor's at ~60%, and Highschool with the least percentage of approved Visas at less than 40%.

3. Is there a significant difference in visa approval rates between employees with and without prior job experience?

In [191]:
# Creating a table for visa approval by job experience
job_exp_counts = data.groupby(['has_job_experience', 'case_status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
job_exp_counts
Out[191]:
case_status Certified Denied
has_job_experience
N 5994 4684
Y 11024 3778
In [192]:
# Converting the counts to percentage
job_exp_pct = job_exp_counts.div(job_exp_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0) * 100
job_exp_pct
Out[192]:
case_status Certified Denied
has_job_experience
N 56.134 43.866

In [193]:
# Creating a barplot for visa approval by job experience
order = job_exp_pct.index.tolist()
x = np.arange(len(order))
width = 0.35

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

certified = plt.bar(
    x - width/2,
    job_exp_pct.loc[order, 'Certified'],
    width,
    label='Certified'
)

denied = plt.bar(
    x + width/2,
    job_exp_pct.loc[order, 'Denied'],
    width,
    label='Denied'
)

plt.xticks(x, order)
plt.xlabel("Has Job Experience (Y/N)")
plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")
plt.title("Visa Approval Rates by Prior Job Experience")
plt.legend(title="Case Status")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

  • There is a marked difference in visa approval between those who did and did not have previous work experience.

  • 74% of employees that had experience were approved while just 56% of employees without experience were approved.

4. How does the prevailing wage affect visa approval? Do higher wages lead to higher chances of approval?

In [194]:
# Showing boxplot of wage vs. case status
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
data.boxplot(column='prevailing_wage', by='case_status')
plt.title("Prevailing Wage vs Case Status")
plt.suptitle("")
plt.xlabel("Case Status")
plt.ylabel("Prevailing Wage")
plt.show()

<Figure size 1000x600 with 0 Axes>
  • This visualization is highly skewed from the many extreme outliers and not effective for interpretation. Breaking the wages into bins will make it more effective.
In [195]:
# Creating bins based on quartiles
data['wage_bin'] = pd.qcut(
    data['prevailing_wage'],
    q=4,
    labels=['Low', 'Medium', 'High', 'Very High']
)
In [196]:
# Calculating approval rates within each bin
wage_status_counts = data.groupby(['wage_bin', 'case_status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
wage_status_pct = wage_status_counts.div(wage_status_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0) * 100
wage_status_pct
Out[196]:
case_status Certified Denied
wage_bin
Low 71.162 28.838
Medium 69.639 30.361
High 68.477 31.523
Very High 57.881 42.119
In [197]:
# Making a stacked barplot of Cases status by Wage level
wage_status_pct.plot(
    kind='bar',
    stacked=True,
    figsize=(10,6)
)

plt.title("Visa Approval Rates by Wage Level")
plt.xlabel("Wage Level")
plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")
plt.legend(title="Case Status")
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • The first three quartile bins of wages (Low, Medium and High) have the higher approval rates close to 70% while the approval rates for Very High prevailing wage is about 60%. It does appear to be a factor in Visa approval.

5. Do certain regions in the US have higher visa approval rates compared to others?

In [257]:
# Making a stacked barplot of Case status by Region
region_pct.plot(
    kind='bar',
    stacked=True,
    figsize=(10,6)
)

plt.title("Visa Approval Rates by US Region")
plt.xlabel("US Region")
plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")
plt.legend(title="Case Status")
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • The region with the highest percentage of Visa approvals was Midwest with 75%.
  • The South had the second highest Visa approval at 70%.
  • Island had the least amount of Visa approvals at about 60%.
  • The West and Northeast had similarly low Visa Approvals at about 62% each.

6. How does the number of employees in a company influence visa approval? Do larger companies have a higher approval rate?

In [200]:
# Creating company size buckets based on quartiles
bins = [0, 1022, 2109, 3504, float('inf')]
labels = ['Small', 'Medium', 'Large', 'Very Large']

data['company_size'] = pd.cut(
    data['no_of_employees'],
    bins=bins,
    labels=labels,
    include_lowest=True
)
In [201]:
# Computing approval percentages
size_counts = data.groupby(['company_size', 'case_status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
size_pct = size_counts.div(size_counts.sum(axis=1), axis=0) * 100
size_pct
Out[201]:
case_status Certified Denied
company_size
Small 65.994 34.006
Medium 65.609 34.391
Large 66.039 33.961
Very Large 69.593 30.407
In [258]:
# Making a stacked barplot of Cases status by Wage level
size_pct.plot(
    kind='bar',
    stacked=True,
    figsize=(10,6)
)

plt.title("Visa Approval Rates by Company Size")
plt.xlabel("Company Size")
plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")
plt.legend(title="Case Status")
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • The largest gap in approval rate was between very large at ~69% and Medium at ~65%. This implies that larger companies have a higher approval rate, but not by much.

7. Are visa approval rates different across various continents of employees? Which continent has the highest and lowest approval rates?

In [203]:
# Getting counts of Visa approval by continent
continent_counts = data.groupby(['continent', 'case_status']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
continent_counts

continent_pct
Out[204]:
case_status Certified Denied
continent
Africa 72.051 27.949

In [259]:
# Making a stacked barplot of Cases status by Wage level
continent_pct.plot(
    kind='bar',
    stacked=True,
    figsize=(10,6)
)

plt.title("Visa Approval Rates by Continent")
plt.xlabel("Continent")
plt.ylabel("Percentage (%)")
plt.legend(title="Case Status")
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

  • The Visa approval rates across continents vary widely from almost 80% to a low of about 57%.

  • The leading continents with the highest approval rate is Europe at 79%, Africa at 72%, and Asia at 65%.

  • The continents with the lowest approval rate are Oceania at 63%, North America at 61%, and South America at 57%.

Univariate Analysis¶

In [205]:
# Creating outline for Boxplot and Histogram
def histogram_boxplot(data, feature, figsize=(15, 10), kde=False, bins=None):
    """
    Boxplot and histogram combined

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    figsize: size of figure (default (15,10))
    kde: whether to show the density curve (default False)
    bins: number of bins for histogram (default None)
    """
    f2, (ax_box2, ax_hist2) = plt.subplots(
        nrows=2,  # Number of rows of the subplot grid= 2
        sharex=True,  # x-axis will be shared among all subplots
        gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": (0.25, 0.75)},
        figsize=figsize,
    )  # creating the 2 subplots
    sns.boxplot(
        data=data, x=feature, ax=ax_box2, showmeans=True, color="violet"
    )  # boxplot will be created and a triangle will indicate the mean value of the column
    sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2, bins=bins
    ) if bins else sns.histplot(
        data=data, x=feature, kde=kde, ax=ax_hist2
    )  # For histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].mean(), color="green", linestyle="--"
    )  # Add mean to the histogram
    ax_hist2.axvline(
        data[feature].median(), color="black", linestyle="-"
    )  # Add median to the histogram

Observations on no_of_employees

In [206]:
# Calling histogram + boxplot for number of employees
histogram_boxplot(data, "no_of_employees")
  • The distribution is extremely right-skewed.
  • Very few companies have tens of thousands or hundreds of thousands of employees, creating extreme outliers.

Observations on yr_of_estab

In [207]:
# Calling histogram + boxplot for year established
histogram_boxplot(data, "yr_of_estab")
  • The observations are heavily left-skewed.
  • There are many outliers of companies being established in the 1800s and early 1900s.
  • At least 50% of the companies were established between 1980 to 2004.

Observations on prevailing_wage

In [208]:
# Calling histogram + boxplot for prevailing_wage
histogram_boxplot(data, "prevailing_wage")
  • The distribution is highly right-skewed.
  • There are very few employees with extremely high prevailing wages creating extreme outliers.
  • This data could be more effectively visualized if log-transformed.

Observations on continent

In [209]:
# function to create labeled barplots


def labeled_barplot(data, feature, perc=False, n=None):
    """
    Barplot with percentage at the top

    data: dataframe
    feature: dataframe column
    perc: whether to display percentages instead of count (default is False)
    n: displays the top n category levels (default is None, i.e., display all levels)
    """

    total = len(data[feature])  # length of the column
    count = data[feature].nunique()
    if n is None:
        plt.figure(figsize=(count + 1, 5))
    else:
        plt.figure(figsize=(n + 1, 5))

    plt.xticks(rotation=90, fontsize=15)
    ax = sns.countplot(
        data=data,
        x=feature,
        palette="Paired",
        order=data[feature].value_counts().index[:n].sort_values(),
    )

    for p in ax.patches:
        if perc == True:
            label = "{:.1f}%".format(
                100 * p.get_height() / total
            )  # percentage of each class of the category
        else:
            label = p.get_height()  # count of each level of the category

        x = p.get_x() + p.get_width() / 2  # width of the plot
        y = p.get_height()  # height of the plot

        ax.annotate(
            label,
            (x, y),
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            size=12,
            xytext=(0, 5),
            textcoords="offset points",
        )  # annotate the percentage

    plt.show()  # show the plot
In [210]:
# Calling barplot for continent
labeled_barplot(data, "continent", perc=True)
  • Most of the observations are on employees from Asia at 66.2%.
  • North America (12.9%) and Europe(14.6%) are the next leading groups of employees.
  • Oceania has the lowest at 0.8% of the observations.

Observations on education_of_employee

  • The minority of employees at 8.6% have a Doctorate.

continent vs prevailing_wage

    x="continent",
    y="prevailing_wage",
    palette="Blues_r"

plt.ylabel("Median Prevailing Wage")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [220]:
# Calling log transformed boxplot

sns.boxplot(x="continent", y="prevailing_wage", data=data)
plt.yscale("log")
plt.title("Prevailing Wage by Continent (Log Scale)")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
  • Since prevailing_wage has extreme outliers, we're looking at the median values to get a more typical representation.
  • The median prevailing wages for Africa and Asia are the highest at about 75,000. Europe has the lowest median prevailing wage at about 50,000.
  • These numbers are consistent with the log transformed boxplot With Africa and Asia in the top among the highest median wage with North America and South America, while Europe has the lowest.

region_of_employment vs prevailing wage

# Set X and y variables
X = data1.drop(["case_status"], axis=1)
y = data1["case_status"].apply(lambda x: 1 if x == "Certified" else 0)
In [297]:
# Splitting data into training, validation and test set:
# first we split data into 2 parts, say temporary and test

X_temp, X_test, y_temp, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

# then we split the temporary set into train and validation

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_temp, y_temp, test_size=0.25, random_state=1, stratify=y_temp
)
print(X_train.shape, X_val.shape, X_test.shape)

In [231]:
# defining a function to compute different metrics to check performance of a classification model built using sklearn
def model_performance_classification_sklearn(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Function to compute different metrics to check classification model performance

    model: classifier
    predictors: independent variables
    target: dependent variable
    """

    # predicting using the independent variables
    pred = model.predict(predictors)

    acc = accuracy_score(target, pred)  # to compute Accuracy
    recall = recall_score(target, pred)  # to compute Recall
    precision = precision_score(target, pred)  # to compute Precision
    f1 = f1_score(target, pred)  # to compute F1-score

    # creating a dataframe of metrics
    df_perf = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Accuracy": acc, "Recall": recall, "Precision": precision, "F1": f1,},
        index=[0],
    )

    return df_perf

Model Building¶

In [281]:
models = []  # Empty list to store all the models

# Appending models into the list
models.append(("Bagging", BaggingClassifier(estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced'), random_state=1)))
models.append(("Random forest", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))
models.append(("GBM", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("Adaboost", AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)))
models.append(("dtree", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1, class_weight='balanced')))

print("\nTraining Performance:\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    scores = recall_score(y_train, model.predict(X_train))
    print("{}: {}".format(name, scores))

print("\nValidation Performance:\n")
for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    scores_val = recall_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
    print("{}: {}".format(name, scores_val))

Training Performance:

Bagging: 0.9886385896180215
Random forest: 1.0
GBM: 0.8765915768854065
Adaboost: 0.8877571008814887
dtree: 1.0

Validation Performance:

Bagging: 0.7855464159811986
Random forest: 0.8437132784958872
GBM: 0.8710340775558166
Adaboost: 0.8816098707403055
dtree: 0.7391304347826086
In [282]:
print("\nTraining and Validation Performance Difference:\n")

for name, model in models:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    scores_train = recall_score(y_train, model.predict(X_train))
    scores_val = recall_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
    difference1 = scores_train - scores_val
    print("{}: Training Score: {:.4f}, Validation Score: {:.4f}, Difference: {:.4f}".format(name, scores_train, scores_val, difference1))

Training and Validation Performance Difference:

Bagging: Training Score: 0.9886, Validation Score: 0.7855, Difference: 0.2031
Random forest: Training Score: 1.0000, Validation Score: 0.8437, Difference: 0.1563
GBM: Training Score: 0.8766, Validation Score: 0.8710, Difference: 0.0056
Adaboost: Training Score: 0.8878, Validation Score: 0.8816, Difference: 0.0061
dtree: Training Score: 1.0000, Validation Score: 0.7391, Difference: 0.2609
  • GBM performed the best with the highest, most stable Validation score, followed by AdaBoost.

Model Building - Oversampled Data¶

In [283]:
print("Before Oversampling, counts of label 'Certified': {}".format(sum(y_train == 1)))
print("Before Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': {} \n".format(sum(y_train == 0)))

sm = SMOTE(
    sampling_strategy=1, k_neighbors=5, random_state=1
)  # Synthetic Minority Over Sampling Technique
X_train_over, y_train_over = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)


print("After Oversampling, counts of label 'Certified': {}".format(sum(y_train_over == 1)))
print("After Oversampling, counts of label 'Denied': {} \n".format(sum(y_train_over == 0)))


print("After Oversampling, the shape of train_X: {}".format(X_train_over.shape))
print("After Oversampling, the shape of train_y: {} \n".format(y_train_over.shape))

  • GBM performed the best, followed by Adaboost, according to the validation performance.

Model Performance Improvement¶

  • After running 15 models from the original data, oversampled and undersampled data, AdaBoost and GBM models trained on oversampled data and GBM trained on undersampled data were the top peformers in both training and validation sets.
  • In order to reduce class bias, I will not be hypertuning or using Adaboost or GBM from the original data set, even though they performed exceptionally well.

Tuning AdaBoostClassifier model with Oversampled Data¶

In [290]:
%%time

# defining model
Model = AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)

# Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "n_estimators": np.arange(50,110,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01,0.1,0.05],
    "estimator": [
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, random_state=1),
        DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=1),
    ],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.recall_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_jobs = -1, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'n_estimators': np.int64(50), 'learning_rate': 0.01, 'estimator': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1)} with CV score=0.9313418217433889:
CPU times: user 2.94 s, sys: 1.25 s, total: 4.19 s
Wall time: 4min 45s

    random_state=1,
    n_estimators=50,
    learning_rate=0.01,
    estimator=DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, random_state=1),
)
tuned_adb.fit(X_train_over, y_train_over)

Accuracy Recall Precision F1

adb_val
Out[302]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.716 0.934 0.722 0.815
  • The Recall and F1 score improved slightly but overall remained stable.

Tuning Gradient Boosting model with Oversampled Data¶

In [303]:
%%time

#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(50,110,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01, 0.1, 0.05],
    "subsample":[0.7,0.9],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],
}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.recall_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_over,y_train_over)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 0.9, 'n_estimators': np.int64(100), 'max_features': 1, 'learning_rate': 0.01, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.867482859941234:

Tuning Gradient Boosting model with Undersampled Data¶

In [313]:
%%time

#Creating pipeline
Model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=1)

#Parameter grid to pass in RandomSearchCV
param_grid = {
    "init": [AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1),DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=1)],
    "n_estimators": np.arange(50,110,25),
    "learning_rate": [0.01,0.1,0.05],
    "subsample":[0.7,0.9],
    "max_features":[0.5,0.7,1],}

# Type of scoring used to compare parameter combinations
scorer = metrics.make_scorer(metrics.recall_score)

#Calling RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv = RandomizedSearchCV(estimator=Model, param_distributions=param_grid, n_iter=50, scoring=scorer, cv=5, random_state=1, n_jobs = -1)

#Fitting parameters in RandomizedSearchCV
randomized_cv.fit(X_train_un,y_train_un)

print("Best parameters are {} with CV score={}:" .format(randomized_cv.best_params_,randomized_cv.best_score_))

Best parameters are {'subsample': 0.9, 'n_estimators': np.int64(100), 'max_features': 1, 'learning_rate': 0.05, 'init': AdaBoostClassifier(random_state=1)} with CV score=0.7469481401031768:
CPU times: user 2.74 s, sys: 306 ms, total: 3.05 s
Wall time: 2min 49s

Model Comparison and Final Model Selection¶

In [318]:
# training performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [
        adb_train.T,
        gbm_train.T,
        gbm2_train.T,
    ],
    axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data",
]
print("Training performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Training performance comparison:
Out[318]:
AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data
Accuracy 0.647 1.000 0.704
Recall 0.931 1.000 0.743
Precision 0.594 1.000 0.689
F1 0.725 1.000 0.715
In [319]:
# Validation performance comparison

models_train_comp_df = pd.concat(
    [adb_val.T, gbm_val.T, gbm2_val.T], axis=1,
)
models_train_comp_df.columns = [
    "AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data",
    "Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data"
]
print("Validation performance comparison:")
models_train_comp_df

Validation performance comparison:
Out[319]:
AdaBoost trained with Oversampled data Gradient boosting trained with Oversampled data Gradient boosting trained with Undersampled data
Accuracy 0.716 0.665 0.717
Recall 0.934 0.743 0.735
Precision 0.722 0.753 0.823
F1 0.815 0.748 0.776
  • AdaBoost trained with oversampled data remained the most stable in terms of recall so I consider this the best model.
In [322]:
# Let's check the performance on test set
ada_test = model_performance_classification_sklearn(tuned_adb, X_test, y_test)
ada_test
Out[322]:
Accuracy Recall Precision F1
0 0.708 0.930 0.717 0.810
  • AdaBoost returned the test set with a 93% recall which is consistent with recalls achieved with training and validation sets.
  • This indicates that this model generalizes well.
In [321]:
# Compute feature importances
feature_names = X_train.columns
importances = tuned_adb.feature_importances_
indices = np.argsort(importances)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 12))
plt.title("Feature Importances")
plt.barh(range(len(indices)), importances[indices], color="violet", align="center")
plt.yticks(range(len(indices)), [feature_names[i] for i in indices])
plt.xlabel("Relative Importance")
plt.show()
  • Feature importances indicate that High School education, Master's level education and previous job experience are major determining factors in the Visa approval process.

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

  • Education level plays a significant role in whether an applicant's Visa is Certified or Denied. Those will only a High School Education were significantly less likely to be approved, while those holding a Master's or above had the highest rates of approval.

  • Prior job experience is also a key factor separating Certified versus Denied applications. Individuals demonstrating work history significantly increase their chances of Visa approval.

  • EasyVisa's profile could prioritize applications combining higher education and previous work experience, while flagging applicants that are only High School educated or have no work experience. This will help streamline the process by focusing first on candidates that are most likely to be approved.

from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')

from google.colab import files

notebook_name = "/content/drive/MyDrive/ColabNotebooks/AARodriguez_ProjectFullCodeNB_EasyVisa.ipynbRodriguez_Project_SLC_DSBA_INNHotels_FullCode.ipynb"
html_output_name = notebook_name.replace('.ipynb', '.html')

get_ipython().system(f'jupyter nbconvert --to html "{notebook_name}"')
files.download(html_output_name)
